message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The respiratory virus responsible for Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19), Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-2), has impacted nearly every aspect of life worldwide, claiming the lives of over 2.5 million people globally, at the time of this publication. Neutralizing nanobodies (V
                  <jats:sub>H</jats:sub>
                  H) represent a promising therapeutic intervention strategy to address the current SARS-2 pandemic and provide a powerful toolkit to address future virus outbreaks. Using a synthetic, high-diversity V
                  <jats:sub>H</jats:sub>
                  H bacteriophage library, several potent neutralizing V
                  <jats:sub>H</jats:sub>
                  H antibodies were identified and evaluated for their capacity to tightly bind to the SARS-2 receptor-binding domain (RBD), to prevent binding of SARS-2 spike (S) to the cellular receptor Angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2), and to neutralize viral infection. Preliminary preclinical evaluation of multiple nanobody candidates demonstrate that they are prophylactically and therapeutically effective
                  <jats:italic>in vivo</jats:italic>
                  against
                  <jats:italic>wildtype</jats:italic>
                  SARS-2. The identified and characterized nanobodies described herein represent viable candidates for further preclinical evaluation and another tool to add to our therapeutic arsenal to address the COVID-19 pandemic.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Author Summary</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    To fully address the on-going pandemic caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-2), it will be important to have both vaccines and therapeutic strategies to prevent and mitigate the effects of SARS-2. In this study, we describe the identification and characterization of potently neutralizing humanized single domain heavy chain (V
                    <jats:sub>H</jats:sub>
                    H) antibodies that have binding affinity for both the original Wuhan strain and widely circulating B.1.1.7/UK strain. V
                    <jats:sub>H</jats:sub>
                    H antibodies have the same therapeutic potential as conventional antibodies in half the size and with greater stability and solubility. Using a synthetic humanized high-diversity V
                    <jats:sub>H</jats:sub>
                    H phage library we identified several candidates with strong affinity for the SARS-2 spike that block the interaction of SARS-2 spike with the cellular receptor ACE2, and effectively neutralize infection with SARS-2
                    <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                    . By sequencing viral escape mutants generated in the presence of each V
                    <jats:sub>H</jats:sub>
                    H we mapped the binding sites of the V
                    <jats:sub>H</jats:sub>
                    H antibodies and assessed their affinity against newly emerging SARS-2 variants. Finally, we demonstrate that two of these V
                    <jats:sub>H</jats:sub>
                    H antibodies show prophylactic and therapeutic efficacy
                    <jats:italic>in vivo</jats:italic>
                    against challenge with SARS-2. This study establishes that screening highly diverse V
                    <jats:sub>H</jats:sub>
                    H phage libraries against viral threats can yield highly effective therapeutic agents in real time.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Development of Potent and Effective Synthetic SARS-CoV-2 Neutralizing Nanobodies
